stem: A bitmap image is 800 pixels wide and 600 pixels tall. The image uses a colour depth of 24 bits per pixel.
model_answer: File size in bits = width × height × colour depth = 800 × 600 × 24 = 11 520 000 bits. Increasing the colour depth (e.g. to 32 bpp) records more colour information per pixel, so each pixel needs more bits and the file gets larger; reducing the colour depth makes the file smaller but limits the number of distinct colours that can be shown.
review_notes: Teacher-marked. Award the calculation (formula or final value), and an explanation of how colour depth changes file size. Convert to bytes is acceptable: 11 520 000 / 8 = 1 440 000 bytes ≈ 1.44 MB.

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Explain how to calculate the file size of this image and how the file size would change if the colour depth was increased.

mark_points.text: file size = width × height × colour depth (bits) (or equivalent worked calculation)
uses the values to reach 11 520 000 bits / 1 440 000 bytes / ≈ 1.44 MB
increasing colour depth increases file size (more bits per pixel)

misconceptions.label: Forgets the colour depth term
misconceptions.description: Calculating only width × height gives pixels, not bits. Mark scheme requires multiplication by colour depth.
